revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Japanese burial mounds]] to [[Category:kofun]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}

revision.text:
[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.

revision.text:
[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:Yayoi period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Makimuku site]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Makimuku-style keyhole-shaped tumulus]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Makimuku-style keyhole-shaped tumulus]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped burial mounds]][[category:Makimuku-style keyhole-shaped tumulus]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped burial mounds]][[Category:pp]][[category:Makimuku-style keyhole-shaped tumulus]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped burial mounds]][[category:Makimuku-style keyhole-shaped tumulus]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped burial mounds]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped burial mounds]][[category:Makimuku-style keyhole-shaped tumulus]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped burial mounds]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Makimuku-style keyhole-shaped tumulus]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:纒向型前方後円墳]]

[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}

== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.

However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}

[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}

revision.text:
[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.

While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.

Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.

revision.text:
[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.

For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}
== References == <references />

revision.text:
[[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群}} in {{ill|Ichihara|ja|市原市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫}}. This term describes certain burial mounds with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} from the late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} or as early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the {{ill|Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳}}, {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳}}, tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳}} in {{ill|Ogōri|ja|小郡市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These burial mounds feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}} and {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}}, and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} to northern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}, and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling {{ill|Rammed earth|lt=rammed-earth construction|ja|版築|en}}, rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), pp. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳}}—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar burial mounds across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築墳丘墓}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the {{ill|Saijō Kofun Cluster|ja|西条古墳群}} in {{ill|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市}}, Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi burial mounds and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the {{ill|Tatetsuki Burial Mound|ja|楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site}} in {{ill|Kurashiki|ja|倉敷市}}, Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}}, further emphasizes the influence of {{ill|Kibi Province|ja|吉備国}}. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), pp. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi burial mounds<ref>Hirose (2003), pp. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳}} indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡}} * {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)
