revision.comment: Text replacement - "Special platforms and jars" to "Ceremonial vessel stands or jars"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11608504]] Wikidata name: Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōdo Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in [[:en:Ichihara, Chiba|Ichihara]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terasawa Kaoru|qq=|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}}. This term describes certain Kofuns with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yayoi-mound burial sites|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} from the late [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] or as early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]], [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun]], tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukonamaegake Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ogōri]], Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These Kofuns feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]], and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] to northern [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]], and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period pottery|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling [[:en:Rammed earth|rammed-earth construction]], rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the [[:en:Saijō Kofun Cluster|Saijō Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Kakogawa, Hyōgo|Kakogawa]], Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi Kofuns and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|qq=|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

This term describes certain Kofuns with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yayoi-mound burial sites|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} from the late [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] or as early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]], [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun]], tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukonamaegake Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ogōri]], Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These Kofuns feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]], and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] to northern [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]], and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period pottery|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling [[:en:Rammed earth|rammed-earth construction]], rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the [[:en:Saijō Kofun Cluster|Saijō Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Kakogawa, Hyōgo|Kakogawa]], Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi Kofuns and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|qq=|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|qq=|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]

revision.comment: Bot: categorize based on Wikidata P11250 property
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11608504]] Wikidata name: Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōdo Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in [[:en:Ichihara, Chiba|Ichihara]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terasawa Kaoru|qq=|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}}. This term describes certain Kofuns with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yayoi-mound burial sites|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} from the late [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] or as early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]], [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun]], tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukonamaegake Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ogōri]], Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These Kofuns feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]], and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] to northern [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]], and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period pottery|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling [[:en:Rammed earth|rammed-earth construction]], rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the [[:en:Saijō Kofun Cluster|Saijō Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Kakogawa, Hyōgo|Kakogawa]], Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi Kofuns and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|qq=|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the [[:en:Saijō Kofun Cluster|Saijō Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Kakogawa, Hyōgo|Kakogawa]], Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi Kofuns and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|qq=|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:纒向型前方後円墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11608504]] Wikidata name: Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōdo Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in [[:en:Ichihara, Chiba|Ichihara]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terasawa Kaoru|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}}. This term describes certain Kofuns with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yayoi-mound burial sites|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} from the late [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] or as early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]], [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun]], tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ogōri]], Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These Kofuns feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]], and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] to northern [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]], and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period pottery|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling [[:en:Rammed earth|rammed-earth construction]], rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the [[:en:Saijō Kofun Cluster|Saijō Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Kakogawa, Hyōgo|Kakogawa]], Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi Kofuns and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:纒向型前方後円墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11608504]] Wikidata name: Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōdo Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in [[:en:Ichihara, Chiba|Ichihara]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terasawa Kaoru|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}}. This term describes certain Kofuns with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yayoi-mound burial sites|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} from the late [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] or as early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]], [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun]], tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ogōri]], Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These Kofuns feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]], and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] to northern [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]], and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period pottery|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling [[:en:Rammed earth|rammed-earth construction]], rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the [[:en:Saijō Kofun Cluster|Saijō Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Kakogawa, Hyōgo|Kakogawa]], Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi Kofuns and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:纒向型前方後円墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11608504]] Wikidata name: Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōdo Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in [[:en:Ichihara, Chiba|Ichihara]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terasawa Kaoru|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}}. This term describes certain Kofuns with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yayoi-mound burial sites|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} from the late [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] or as early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]], [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun]], tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ogōri]], Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These Kofuns feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]], and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] to northern [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]], and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period pottery|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling [[:en:Rammed earth|rammed-earth construction]], rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the [[:en:Saijō Kofun Cluster|Saijō Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Kakogawa, Hyōgo|Kakogawa]], Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi Kofuns and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the [[:en:Saijō Kofun Cluster|Saijō Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Kakogawa, Hyōgo|Kakogawa]], Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi Kofuns and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:纒向型前方後円墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11608504]] Wikidata name: Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōdo Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in [[:en:Ichihara, Chiba|Ichihara]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terasawa Kaoru|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}}. This term describes certain Kofuns with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yayoi-mound burial sites|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} from the late [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] or as early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]], [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun]], tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ogōri]], Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These Kofuns feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]], and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] to northern [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]], and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period pottery|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling [[:en:Rammed earth|rammed-earth construction]], rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]

The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.

Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:纒向型前方後円墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11608504]] Wikidata name: Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Godo Kofun Group-5, funkyu.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Example of a Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun: Tomb No. 5 of the {{ill|Gōdo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōdo Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in [[:en:Ichihara, Chiba|Ichihara]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].]] '''Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun''' (纒向型前方後円墳, literally "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped tumulus") refers to a concept proposed by {{ill|Terasawa Kaoru|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terasawa Kaoru|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}}. This term describes certain Kofuns with keyhole shapes initially classified as {{ill|yayoi-mound burial sites|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yayoi-mound burial sites|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} from the late [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] or as early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] emergence mounds. The concept was suggested to evaluate these mounds more positively as true [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] based on excavation and research.
== Overview == Kaoru Terasawa from the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] proposed the concept in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Examples of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun include the [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]], [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yatsuzuka Kofun]], tombs No. 4 and No. 5 of the Gōdo Kofun Cluster in Ichihara, Chiba Prefecture<ref>According to the Japan Quaternary Society's *Illustrated Human Sites of Japan* (1992), Tomb No. 5 (39 meters long) dates to the early Yayoi Phase VI, and Tomb No. 4 (49 meters long) to the later Yayoi Phase VI.</ref>, and the {{ill|Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja|津古生掛古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukonamaegake Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ogōri]], Fukuoka Prefecture<ref>Tsukonamaegake Kofun has a mound approximately 33 meters long, with artifacts such as 57 beads, one mirror, a sword, and ridge-edged arrowheads.</ref>. These Kofuns feature a rectangular extension attached to a circular main mound.
Terasawa observed that these mounds predate and coincide with the establishment of standardized zenpō-kōen-fun, represented by the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]], and formed under the political and ceremonial influence of the central Makimuku site associated with early Yamato rule<ref name=tera1>Terasawa (1988).</ref>.
Such mounds are characterized by the use of Chinese measurement systems, standardization across regions from [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] to northern [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]], and their association with {{ill|Kofun period pottery|ja|畿内型土器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period pottery|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The construction methods often employed a framework with compacted soil, resembling [[:en:Rammed earth|rammed-earth construction]], rather than simple soil mounding. A distinct feature is the short, low rectangular front section, with ratios of mound length to rear and front sections of approximately 3:2:1<ref name=tera2>Terasawa (1994), 11. 28-31.</ref>. Terasawa argued that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun developed by increasing the scale and distinctiveness of these elements<ref name=tera1 />.
Key examples within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]—such as Ishizuka, Yatsuzuka, and the [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]]—share common features, including mound lengths of 90-100 meters, short front sections, and scallop-like shapes<ref>The term "scallop-shaped" (帆立貝式) describes zenpō-kōen-fun with a front section that does not extend straight out. While this shape persists across periods, shape alone is insufficient for precise dating.</ref>. Later research revealed similar Kofuns across a wide range of Japan, featuring elements predating standardized kofun.
[[Image:Tatetsuki remains.JPG|280px|thumb|right|{{ill|Tatetsuki Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatetsuki Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541408}}, with a distinct array of standing stones.]]
Terasawa positioned the advent of the Kofun period as marked by the emergence of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun and their associated societies. He identified early prototypes such as Tomb No. 52 of the [[:en:Saijō Kofun Cluster|Saijō Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Kakogawa, Hyōgo|Kakogawa]], Hyōgo Prefecture<ref>Terasawa (1988).</ref>. Tomb No. 52, measuring approximately 25 meters in mound length, combines features of Yayoi Kofuns and zenpō-kōen-fun. Its construction is dated to the early 3rd century in a report issued in 2009<ref>Excavations were conducted between 1963 and 1964, with the Harima Archaeology Research Group publishing its findings in 2009.</ref>.
The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.city.ichihara.chiba.jp/~maibun/isekifile65.htm Ichihara City Archaeology: Godo Kofun Group] (in Japanese) * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/from-site/2000/hokeno.html Hokenoyama Kofun Excavation Overview] (in Japanese)

The origins of the "Makimuku-type" can be traced to burial practices at the [[:en:Tatetsuki Site|Tatetsuki Site]] in [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], Okayama Prefecture, from the late 2nd century. These mounds feature circular bases with a clear rectangular protrusion one-third their size, representing a precursor to zenpō-kōen-fun<ref name=tera3/>. The use of unique pottery, such as {{ill|specialized pedestal stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized pedestal stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}}, further emphasizes the influence of [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]. The Makimuku site consolidated these elements by the early 3rd century as the capital of the nascent Yamato state.
Terasawa's research demonstrated that standardized zenpō-kōen-fun did not emerge instantaneously but evolved from these early forms, earning high praise for advancing the study of early Kofun period archaeology<ref>Habuta (1995), p. 7.</ref>. However, opinions vary regarding the conceptual leap from Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun to standardized forms<ref>Mochizuki (1990), p. 35.</ref>. Some scholars categorize these as "Makimuku keyhole-shaped trench tombs" rather than distinct kofun<ref>Wada (2004), 11. 174-176.</ref>, while others include them as Yayoi Kofuns<ref>Hirose (2003), 11. 119-121.</ref>.
== Chronology of Makimuku-type Zenpō-kōen-fun == The absolute chronology of Makimuku-type zenpō-kōen-fun relies on precise dating methods, such as dendrochronology. For instance, hinoki (cypress) planks excavated from the moat of the Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun were dated to AD 177. The felling year was determined to be no later than AD 200, indicating the moat was constructed in the late 2nd century<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123, based on Mitsutani (2000).</ref>. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal samples from the [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] indicates a broader range of AD 55-235<ref>Hirose (2003), p. 123.</ref>.
These findings suggest that the Makimuku Kofun Cluster was constructed from the late 2nd century to the early 3rd century. == See Also == * {{ill|Kaoru Terasawa|ja|寺沢薫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoru Terasawa|1=Kaoru Terasawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457937}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]]
